Description: A community-oriented specialty coffee shop inside The Commons at Christ Episcopal Church, with inviting indoor gathering areas, free Wi-Fi, and an outdoor courtyard suited to working, reading, meeting, or lingering. The counter serves espresso drinks, drip coffee, canned nitro cold brew, smoothies, tea, pastries, breakfast sandwiches, and vegan and gluten-free options. The surrounding building functions as a larger gathering space rather than a conventional standalone café and remains available beyond the coffee counter’s daytime schedule on most days. Free on-site parking and several building entrances make it easy to access, while church services, meetings, and community programming may cause the atmosphere to vary throughout the week.
Comments:
So like, I don’t know if I have the vocabulary to articulate how nice this place is. Let me use an example. If you’ve been to Vavela Cafe, it stands out because it’s so polished, fancy looking, almost royal looking. This place is equally as impressive, but in a completely different way. It feels like a rich person’s expansive living room with a courtyard. So not in the fancy, glossy-floor sort of way, but in the extremely comfortable sofas, couches, armchairs, plants sitting on little lamp stands, and pristine coffee tables sort of way.

And it’s huge. This has to be one of the largest coffee shops in the entire city. It’s inside Christ Episcopal Church, but the coffee shop has its own entrance and its own corner, so you won’t be roped into a service, and nobody is standing around waiting to evangelize you. It’s open to the public. Anyone can come hang out. The interior has a ton of tables and a ton of couches. I mean, I’ve never seen this many couches in a coffee shop. This is the coziest of cozy coffee shops in Charlotte. As the creator of this website, I can tell you I’ve been to just about every coffee shop in the city. None of them have this many couches. And all the chairs have padded backs! None of those typical metal uncomfortable coffee shop chairs.

I can’t think of a better place to read a book. Or study. Or work remotely because alongside all the couches are plenty of tables, with little table stands displaying the Wi-Fi password, making it clear they want remote workers around. Access to outlets is good if you sit at a table along the wall, and some of the couches with lamp stands have those outlet tower things nearby, so you can stay charged up. And then there’s the courtyard. It’s very pretty. Lots of flowers, plants, green tables and chairs, and brick walls surrounding you. You could do a photo shoot out there.

I haven’t even talked about the coffee because, to be honest, I don’t drink coffee. But even if it were terrible, which I doubt it is, this place would still be worth visiting. They have tea, which I enjoyed, pastries, some light hot food options, matcha lattes, and all that stuff. But the space is what impresses me so much. And although the coffee shop closes early, the space itself remains open for some time afterward. The church calls it The Commons, and it’s meant to be a community space in general for people to hang out. Bravo to whoever designed this place.

The space has a calm, thoughtfully designed interior with greenery, tree-themed décor, warm seating areas, table seating with outlet access, and a tucked-away feel inside a corporate tower. Comments:
Very comfortable aesthetic. It’s in a corporate office building, but the space was clearly designed to feel cozy in the midst of that. There are plants everywhere, a section with a comfy couch and armchairs, and a few tables with padded seating. I don’t want to call the decor tropical, but with all the plants, bright lighting, and comfortable furniture, it feels lush and inviting. The plants are for sale, by the way.

The food is good. They have coffee, teas, smoothies, snacks, cold drinks, whatever you want. The Italiano sandwich and the bacon, egg, and cheese breakfast sandwich are both solid.

Parking is in the Carillon garage, but they’ll validate it for you, so bring your ticket. The building itself is pretty nice too. There’s a cool mini pond display with some artwork in the lobby. There’s also a gym inside the same space that I think is only for people working in the building, but it’s there. So in the same way the Dowd YMCA has a Giddy Goat location in the lobby that’s open to everyone, this is sort of the same setup.

Description:
A wellness-oriented tea house and community gathering space in Charlotte focused on tea service, tea rituals, seasonal offerings, tastings, workshops, and small-group events. The concept is built around slowing down, being present, and using tea as a way to gather, talk, reflect, and connect with others. Founded by a life and health coach, the space blends a tearoom, calm social lounge, and community workshop setting rather than a standard coffee shop or quick-service café.

Good for quiet cups of tea, relaxed conversation, mindful meetups, personal reflection, tea-centered events, group workshops, and low-key community gatherings. The atmosphere is intentionally slower and more personal, with programming that may include tea rituals, mindfulness, voice and expression workshops, seasonal menu changes, tastings, and special events.
Comments:
Zen vibes here. Drink some tea, chill out. This is a mindfulness centered third place. The chairs are cozy, especially in the sun room. It’s literally in a converted house. It’s compact, so I wouldn’t bring a large group, but to meet a friend, or just sip tea and relax, it’s perfect. There’s parking behind the house, it’s shared by other businesses so it can be easy to miss, but look for the tea signs out front.

I love herbal tea, and the rituals attached to enjoying it in Eastern cultures, where you really unplug from the world and become reflective, so I'm so happy to see that represented here in Charlotte. You come here to slow down, for a quiet reset, to be in the moment, to feel present, not to work on your laptop, study, or worry about life.

Description:
A sister-owned specialty coffee shop in downtown Concord founded by Kayla McBride and Karlee McBride-Webber. Kayla is a five-time WNBA All-Star and guard for the Minnesota Lynx, while Karlee is a former Indiana University Division I basketball player who later worked in coaching, recruiting, and player development in college basketball.

Designed to bring the comfort of a living room into a compact neighborhood café, the space focuses on high-quality specialty drinks, comfortable connection, and relaxed visits. Coffee and tea selections include creative options such as vanilla chai lattes and iced blueberry matcha topped with blueberry cold foam, while its walkable downtown setting makes it well suited to casual meetups and visitors exploring the surrounding district.
Comments: CharlotteFive reports that the shop plans to open by September 2026, while the official Instagram profile describes the timing as fall 2026. You can read more about the shop here.

Comments: The official website lists the opening date as dependent on construction timing, while Axios Charlotte reports that the café could open as early as 2027. See more details here.

Description: A local coffee house in China Grove serving hot and iced coffee drinks, espresso beverages, cold brew, frozen drinks, teas, smoothies, and rotating specialty flavors in a peaceful neighborhood café setting. The space has a cozy, relaxed atmosphere with casual indoor seating, couches, and a comfortable setup for sitting with drinks rather than only ordering to-go. The menu focuses on customizable coffee and non-coffee drinks with a wide range of flavor combinations, making it a flexible stop for sweet specialty drinks, classic coffee, smoothies, and relaxed café visits.
Comments:
If you’ve ever seen No Country for Old Men, this place feels like it could’ve been a replacement set for this scene. Like Anton Chigurh could’ve stopped here in the middle of his hunt to pick up a coffee and interrogate the barista with questions they don’t realize might determine how much longer they’ll be around.

I mean that in a good way. I don’t know if that’s clear, but I do. It feels country. Backroads. Like people come here to talk for hours with Southern accents while the day moves at half speed. I grew up in Seattle, WA, so forgive me if I’m showing my ignorance here, but this felt like a movie set to me in a quaint way.

Aside from that, it’s a small coffee shop with some indoor seating and a little bit outdoors. I’d say this is a good place for locals to hang out. I don’t know that you need to make a day trip from Charlotte just to check it out, but if you’re in the area, it’s solid.

Comments:
Extremely small, but great care was put into decorating the space in a pink, feminine aesthetic with a compact seating arrangement. I certainly wouldn’t suggest coming here to study, work remotely, or have a super secret conversation, but to try the lattes, for sure.

Speaking of lattes, if you’re a latte drinker, please help me understand at what point we can no longer really call it coffee. For example, water is the base for many different kinds of drinks, but if you put enough stuff in it, we just stop calling it water.

The lattes they serve here look incredible. Combinations of stuff I’ve never seen before. Lattes with Oreo cookies on top, muffin crumbles, and every kind of drizzle you can imagine. If you’re into souped up lattes, this is the place for you. Now, seeing as I don't drink coffee, I didn't try any of them, but I did look  upon the menu with awe and wonder.

Comments:
This is like Rally Pickleball on steroids. It’s huge, and locally owned by 2 entrepreneurs from the greater Charlotte area. There’s a restaurant, a coffee shop, a bar, more pickleball courts than I was able to count, and a ton of seating. You don't have to play pickleball to hangout here. You can just visit the various spaces within.

The restaurant-area seating is marked by silverware wrapped in napkins being on those tables. All other seating is free game. The coffee shop, which they call “cafe” on the signage, has bar-style seating, plus some tables and chairs tucked behind the coffee bar facing the indoor pickleball courts. There’s free Wi-Fi, so you can hang out on your laptop and get some work done.

There’s of course food and drink. The coffee isn’t anything incredible, but the coffee shop has cinnamon rolls, which I consider to be the most important thing about this whole place. They have cinnamon rolls. What more do you need to know? They have cinnamon rolls. Praise be 🙌🏽

Description: A specialty Colombian coffee bar inside The Outpost at Lake Norman serving espresso drinks, lattes, cold brew, matcha, chai, Colombian baked goods, pandebonos, cachitos, pastries, and rotating seasonal drinks. The space blends a coffee shop, local retail store, and community gathering area, with an open-concept layout, community tables, cozy seating, decorative wall art, and a kid-friendly area. Comments:
Lots of natural light here thanks to the large windows, good music, and all the seating on the coffee shop side of the building has padded bottoms. Which, to me, is a big deal because I’ve grown tired of the industrial chic metal chairs that all look like this. Even the bar-style seating chairs have padded bottoms, so you can post up here for a while and work remotely, study, hang out with friends, read, shop local on The Outpost side of the building, whatever you want.

There’s a kid-friendly area and plenty of seating throughout the entire space. It was intentionally designed to be community friendly, and you can tell. The space is thoughtfully designed. 2-person tables here and there, then a couch, some armchairs, bar-style seating, and 2 big tables for larger groups. Everyone can come hang out here and find their spot. I love it.

And about the coffee, I don’t drink coffee, but I’m sure it’s good. The owner is Colombian and specifically brews that style of coffee, sourced from her family farm back home, and people who’ve had it before have told me it’s very good. So take that for what it’s worth from a non-coffee drinker.

Comments:
I like this place because I was having a bad day, and the barista, which I’m not actually sure is what you call a bubble tea shop employee, was very bubbly and kind. At a coffee shop they’re baristas, but at a tea shop they’re...what exactly? I don’t know. Anyway, she was very kind, and I felt like I had to return that energy because being all grumpy when someone’s being nice to you sucks. The effort actually made my whole day better.

Aside from that, this place is nice. Great access to outlets, free Wi-Fi, enough seating options, lots of hot and cold teas, plus some Asian food options. The rice balls are good.

Not a bad place to study, work remotely, or meet a friend for some conversation. The seating isn’t exactly cozy. No couches or armchairs, but what they do have is fine. At least the chairs have padded seats.

Address: 4090 Charlotte Hwy, Lake Wylie, SC 29710, USA
Hours: ["Monday: 6 AM - 6 PM", "Tuesday: 6 AM - 6 PM", "Wednesday: 6 AM - 6 PM", "Thursday: 6 AM - 6 PM", "Friday: 6 AM - 6 PM", "Saturday: 7 AM - 3 PM", "Sunday: 7 AM - 6 PM"]
Description: A locally owned bagel shop and café in Lake Wylie serving New York-style bagels, breakfast sandwiches, spreads, gourmet coffee, lunch items, pastries, and hand-scooped ice cream in a cozy neighborhood setting. The menu includes fresh bagels, egg sandwiches, deli-style sandwiches, baked goods, coffee drinks, and sweet treats, making it useful beyond the morning rush. The space has a casual café feel with indoor seating, a welcoming local atmosphere, and room for sitting with coffee, meeting others, or staying a while. Good for bagels, breakfast, coffee, lunch, ice cream, casual conversation, and a relaxed community-focused stop near the lake.
Comments:
This place is cozy. You can actually use that very overused word for this place. Cozy. The main area when you walk in is mostly wooden tables and chairs, but then there are 3 corners to your left with couches, sofas, armchairs, and side tables with lamps on them. So it feels like you’ve just walked into someone’s living room.

The menu here is fantastic. They have all sorts of bagels and really good bagel sandwiches. Like way beyond just a bacon, egg, and cheese on a bagel. They’ll put steak on it, all sorts of sauces, avocado, and whatever else you want. There's also ice cream. But that’s not the most important thing going on here. What is of the utmost importance is that they have cinnamon rolls. 

Glorious, marvelous, stupendous cinnamon rolls. They’re thick. Heavy. Weighty. Not the dainty cinnamon rolls you get from some coffee shop that considers them a sideshow. The ones here are a seasoned amber brown, baked well enough to hold form so as to be a landing place for vanilla icing, but not so long that when warmed, they aren’t still tender. I presume they’re made in house. They’re excellent.

There's a bookcase with a good selection of books to choose from. Books are sourced from The Cozy Book Company in Fort Mill, and they take requests. There's a box you can leave a note in for any book you'd like to see on the shelves. The selection is pretty good, and only drives home the fact that you're meant to stay awhile here. Hangout, eat, read a book, chill.

If you’re in the Steele Creek area, or like cinnamon rolls, or like bagels, or like hanging out in homey cozy spots, or like working remotely from random places, or like having somewhere to hang out after hiking McDowell Nature Preserve, or just like good food, I highly suggest you check this place out.

Comments:
It’s hard to say anything in South End is a hidden gem because of how busy the area is, but in terms of a place to hang out for some time, work on your laptop, and kick back, this spot is definitely underrated within the neighborhood.

Free parking, free Wi-Fi, of course donuts, sometimes cinnamon rolls too, basic coffee options, cold drinks, great access to outlets, and outside of the mornings, it’s hardly ever busy. This store was clearly designed for people to stay a while, the same way you would at a coffee shop, but it seems like very few people realize that.

The music tends to be a bit loud, so bring noise cancelling headphones, but aside from that, it’s a sleeper pick in the area for a place to hang out. I’ve camped out here on my laptop before and enjoyed being the only one around for the time being.

Now, if you’re on a diet or trying to eat healthier, then definitely don’t hang out here. It’s hard to resist a Krispy Kreme donut. And sometimes they have cinnamon rolls, although to be honest, it’s closer to a coffee roll, which isn’t at all the same thing as a cinnamon roll. Trust me. Cinnamon rolls are my thing.

Oh, and if you’re a true big back, they have a 24 hour donut vending machine outdoors, so you can always get your sugar fix. Do what you will with that information.

Address: 2000 South Blvd Ste 300, Charlotte, NC 28203, USA
Hours: ["Monday: 7:30 AM - 6 PM", "Tuesday: 7:30 AM - 6 PM", "Wednesday: 7:30 AM - 6 PM", "Thursday: 7:30 AM - 6 PM", "Friday: 7:30 AM - 6 PM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Sunday: 8 AM - 6 PM"]
Description:
A French-inspired bakery and cafe at Atherton in South End with an all-day menu centered on coffee, tea, pastries, breakfast dishes, sandwiches, salads, and tartines. The space sits along one of South End’s busiest shopping and Rail Trail corridors, making it useful for casual meetups, remote work, brunch, and quick pastry stops.

The atmosphere leans polished and cozy, with a South of France feel, rustic decor, warm cafe seating, blue-and-white accents, and a bakery case built around croissants, cookies, and seasonal pastries. It works well as a daytime cafe for meeting friends, bringing a laptop, grabbing something sweet, or pairing a coffee stop with time around Atherton.
Comments:
They pack a ton of seating into this space. Especially with the outdoor patio, there are just plenty of options for seating. And if there were an award for the third place with the most pillows in Charlotte, this place would win. They have a whole row of seating packed with so many pillows that there’s no way your back will ever go without comfort. So if you have back pain, this might be your third place.

The space has a French countryside aesthetic. So not the Amelie’s eccentric thing with mismatched furniture and chairs and random stuff everywhere. This is more of a polished rustic look. I like it. It’s classy. The menu has a bunch of French sounding stuff, coffee, tea, and a lot of pastries.

Solid place to work remote, especially because Atherton Mill is turning into something of a remote work mainstay for the city. Parking is free, you might have to drive up a few levels in the parking garage to find a spot, but it has enough levels to accommodate the many businesses in the area.

And even if Maman is full, Not Just Coffee is in the same building, so you’ll probably still find somewhere to sit. The NJC here is one of the hottest spots in the city for remote workers, it’s always packed and very social. Maman being here now makes the whole area even more lively. Then you’re also a short walk from Tous Les Jours and SipCha across the street, which are also third places.

If you want to make friends and meet people, this is not a bad area to plop down as an extrovert, which I’m not. For introverts, bring noise cancelling headphones, sit in a corner, and people watch 🙋🏾‍♂️. Also, the music inside Maman can be pretty loud, so if you’re coming to work or study, definitely bring noise cancelling headphones. Great spot, but not necessarily a quiet spot.

I don’t drink coffee, so idk what to tell you about the taste. They do have some good herbal teas though. No cinnamon rolls, but they have something called a Cinnamon Bun Kouign-Amann, which I’ve deduced to be nothing more than a pretentious, Frenchified version of a cinnamon roll. But since they’re too uppity to call it a cinnamon roll, neither will I.

Address: 13129 S Tryon St, Charlotte, NC 28278, USA
Hours: ["Monday: 5 AM - 9 PM", "Tuesday: 5 AM - 9 PM", "Wednesday: 5 AM - 9 PM", "Thursday: 5 AM - 9 PM", "Friday: 5 AM - 9 PM", "Saturday: 5 AM - 9 PM", "Sunday: 5 AM - 9 PM"]
Description: A standard Starbucks coffee shop in the Steele Creek area serving espresso drinks, brewed coffee, cold brew, teas, refreshers, Frappuccinos, breakfast sandwiches, pastries, snacks, and packaged drinks. The space has a typical chain café setup with counter ordering, indoor seating, mobile order pickup, and a familiar menu for quick coffee, light food, and casual sit-down visits. This location is part of a retail center with surface parking and nearby everyday services.
Comments:
I don’t like this location, but I hung out here one Sunday morning before a meeting I had nearby and witnessed it function as a third place for those in the area.

I sat next to an older couple that ostensibly comes in every Sunday morning to do the crossword from the newspaper together, and while they were doing it, a lady sitting next to them jumped in to help them get a particularly hard word. I saw a family come in, take the big table, and catch up with each other while drinking iced coffees. I saw a pair of older dudes who, from the way they talked, seemed like they’d been friends for decades, come in and discuss the latest world news while munching on croissants and drinking black coffee.

So, while I don’t particularly like the layout of this store, I concede it is undoubtedly a third place, and thus I’ve listed it. The seating just feels awkward to me. Most of it either faces the checkout counter, which means you’re constantly looking at everything happening in the store, or it puts your back to the whole room, which means everything is happening behind you. Neither option is great.

Hours: ["Monday: 8 AM - 8 PM", "Tuesday: 8 AM - 8 PM", "Wednesday: 8 AM - 8 PM", "Thursday: 8 AM - 8 PM", "Friday: 8 AM - 8 PM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 8 PM", "Sunday: 9 AM - 6 PM"]
Description: A West Coast-inspired smoothie bowl café in Charlotte’s South End serving açaí bowls, smoothie bowls, smoothies, breakfast tacos, espresso drinks, matcha, chai, coconut coffee, lemonades, and specialty lattes. The menu features buildable fruit bowls with granola, bananas, berries, tropical fruit, nut butters, protein add-ons, coconut, honey, cacao nibs, and other toppings. The interior has a bright beachy feel with colorful branding, counter seating, wood accents, tropical décor, and a clean fast-casual layout centered around the ordering counter and smoothie bowl prep area. The space combines a health-focused café menu with a casual hangout atmosphere for bowls, coffee, and quick meals.
Comments:
On any random weekday between 9 AM - 5 PM it's not that busy here. It's sort of a sleeper pick as a remote work spot in South End. They have free Wi-Fi, coffee, and great access to outlets from the bar seating options. And of course, acai bowls and smoothies and all sorts of fruity stuff. Very tropical aesthetic to the place, bright teal floor, a beach swing, and a garage door looking thing that's usually open so you can walk right in.

Also, side note, I really enjoy how walkable South End is. I've walked here from Atherton Mill after hanging out at Not Just Coffee before. And then you could walk to Common Market, Summit Coffee, and a ton of other spots in this area to hangout. Whoever designed the neighborhood, great job.

Place: Bad Ass Coffee of Hawaii | Rock Hill
Address: 220 W White St, Rock Hill, SC 29730
Hours: ["Monday: 6 AM - 6 PM", "Tuesday: 6 AM - 6 PM", "Wednesday: 6 AM - 6 PM", "Thursday: 6 AM - 6 PM", "Friday: 6 AM - 6 PM", "Saturday: 6 AM - 6 PM", "Sunday: 7 AM - 3 PM"]
Description:
A Hawaiian-themed coffee shop tucked inside The Thread, a converted textile mill in downtown Rock Hill, serving premium Kona and island-grown coffee alongside signature lattes, blended drinks, cold brews, teas, and bags of whole bean and ground coffee to take home. The bright, tropical, Instagram-friendly space offers indoor cafe seating that suits a quick daytime stop, casual remote work, or meeting up with a friend.

Counter service handles espresso drinks and iced beverages kept strong with the brand's frozen coffee cubes, plus a short lineup of food with a Hawaiian twist. The mood is laid-back and family-friendly with a welcoming Aloha spirit, and the downtown setting puts it within walking distance of other shops and offices in the redeveloped mill district.
Comments:
Bright and airy aesthetic, and as the name might imply, there’s a tropical, Hawaiian-inspired vibe in the colors. There’s some outdoor seating as well. Access to outlets is excellent inside. From almost every seating location you can stay charged up.

The menu reminds me of Caribou Coffee in the sense that there’s a little bit of everything. Coffee, tea, cold drinks, sandwiches (but nowhere near as good as Caribou's), pastries, including cinnamon rolls 🙌🏾, and smoothies. The cinnamon rolls are from whatever place the Coffee Republic in Ballantyne gets there's. I can tell. Cinnamon rolls are my thing. They're really good. Seriously, for a coffee shop cinnamon roll its plenty big, generously iced. A quality cinnamon roll without question.

Inside, there are 3 four-person tables, 2 separate lounge areas with 1 coffee table and 4 lounge chairs each, 1 tall group table, and another short group table. So all the seating is built for 4 or more people, meaning if you’re coming solo to work on your laptop or study, you’ll probably end up taking a spot meant for a handful more than just you. But to me that's fine on a warm day because they also have outdoor seating. Everyone can find a place to plop down regardless.

Also, check out the ceiling fans. The blades are shaped like leaves. Lastly, I don’t drink coffee, so I can’t tell you how the coffee tastes, but if you ever make your way down here, let me know.

Address: 20920 Torrence Chapel Rd B6, Cornelius, NC 28031
Hours: ["Monday: 10 AM - 5 PM", "Tuesday: 10 AM - 5 PM", "Wednesday: 10 AM - 5 PM", "Thursday: 10 AM - 5 PM", "Friday: 10 AM - 5 PM", "Saturday: 10 AM - 5 PM", "Sunday: Closed"]
Description: A cozy independent bookstore in Cornelius offering new books, used books, children’s books, puzzles, toys, gifts, and reader-focused retail items in a warm, shelf-filled setting. The store has a strong browsing atmosphere, with winding rows of books, quiet corners, and casual seating that gives visitors space to slow down and explore. It also offers a book trade program, allowing customers to bring in used books for store credit toward future purchases. Good for book browsing, casual reading, children’s books, gift shopping, trade-ins, and spending time in a relaxed neighborhood bookstore.
Comments:
So like, it is literally walls of books, and it’s marvelous. Wonderful. Everywhere you walk, every inch of every wall is covered in books. Seriously. Make sure you turn every corner here, explore the nooks and crannies, the alleys and sideways. Everywhere you’ll find walls of books!

What a lovely place this is. Clearly created by a lover of literature, a bookworms dream. And in all the many corners here, you’ll find chairs. Almost at random, chairs placed all about for people to nestle into. Some comfy armchairs, some cheap plastic ones, walls of books, and chairs here and there for you to sink into among them.

They have free Wi-Fi, but if you’re reading this right now, I forbid you from bringing your laptop here. Read! Read my friend, leave the digital world behind. There’s no end to the books you’ll find here. This is everything a local bookstore should be.

If you’re in Charlotte a day trip to Cornelius to check this place out is worth it. See if you can find my favorite seating spot, an armchair by the front window hidden in a row of books, with some framed news cut outs about the store nearby on the adjacent wall. Find it, and read. Life is better offline. Oh and Caffeto Specialty Coffee is in the same complex, so be sure to check that out as well.

Description:
A craft café and creative wellness studio inside Camp North End offering self-guided arts and crafts, artist-led workshops, group experiences, creative workspace rentals, and community-centered events. The concept works like a café menu, but instead of ordering food, visitors choose from rotating craft experiences with supplies provided, including options like collage, watercolor, keychains, paper crafts, and seasonal projects.

The space is designed for creativity, conversation, and low-pressure gathering, with lounge-style seating, a dedicated workshop area, locally sourced craft and wellness retail items, and a private event setup. Good for creative meetups, small groups, solo crafting, workshops, date nights, private events, and anyone looking for a hands-on third place built around art, wellness, and community.

Hours: ["Monday: 6:30 AM - 3 PM", "Tuesday: 6:30 AM - 3 PM", "Wednesday: 6:30 AM - 3 PM", "Thursday: 6:30 AM - 3 PM", "Friday: 6:30 AM - 3 PM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 3 PM", "Sunday: 8 AM - 3 PM"]
Description:
A specialty coffee shop set inside a fitness and coworking space in South End, serving espresso drinks, a 24-hour cold brew, matcha, chai, and protein smoothies alongside rotating house-made syrups and creative seasonal lattes. The setup is counter-service, with an order-ahead option, and the surrounding coworking environment gives visitors plenty of room to sit, work, or meet up.

The vibe leans bright, community-focused, and productivity-friendly rather than a quiet solo hideaway, making it a solid pick for remote work, casual catching up, or a quick weekday caffeine stop. Locally baked goods are usually on hand, and the drink lineup rotates often with playful seasonal flavors. Weekday mornings open early, and weekend hours run a bit later in the day
Comments:
Free parking at a coffee shop in South End? Halleluiah 🙌🏾. For that alone I'm a fan of this place. Aesthetically, it's bright, light, and airy. Lots of windows letting in natural light, plush seats instead of hard metal chairs to make things feel more comfortable, and plants interspersed throughout the space to give it some color and earthy vibes. And there's two soft couches with comfortable pillows where you can lounge about.

I don’t drink coffee, but they didn’t have herbal tea, so I got an Americano. I didn’t actually know what an Americano was. I just sheepishly admitted that I don’t know how to order coffee and asked for a normal black coffee, and that’s what the barista graciously gave me. I’m very grateful she didn’t make me feel embarrassed. Very kind. Now, I don’t know how an Americano is supposed to taste, but it tasted good to me. Do with that what you will.

As a place to work remotely, this is great. Access to outlets is good, the seating is spread out so you’re not cramped, and there’s even a glass booth where you can take a private call if you really don’t want anyone to hear your conversation. Top tier remote work spot in terms of layout. Whoever designed it clearly wanted to invite laptop campers such as myself, and they succeeded.

The only thing I’ll say is that you really need to bring noise cancelling headphones with you. Downstairs in the same building is a gym, and they have fitness classes blasting music all hours of the day. So, if you’re easily distracted by noise, either bring headphones or skip this spot entirely. Other than that, it’s great.

Hours: ["Monday: 5 PM - 10 PM", "Tuesday: 5 PM - 10 PM", "Wednesday: 11 AM - 10 PM", "Thursday: 11 AM - 10 PM", "Friday: 11 AM - 11 PM", "Saturday: 11 AM - 11 PM", "Sunday: 11 AM - 8 PM"]
Description: A laid-back neighborhood wine and beer spot with an artsy, lived-in feel. The space blends casual bar energy with a bottle-shop sensibility, colorful art, a relaxed indoor area, and outdoor seating that feels more like a backyard gathering than a polished cocktail lounge. Seating is informal and social, with a mix of places to settle in over a drink, drift between conversations, and take in whatever event or art moment is happening that day. The vibe is warm, eclectic, creative, a little quirky, and easygoing.
Comments: Formerly NoDa Company Store. They rebranded.

Comments: Extremely good access to outlets and free wi-fi, so not a bad place at all to plop down and get some work done or study. Its one long hallway really with seating the whole way. I wouldn't call it comfy, except for the corner with the faux fireplace, but it works. There's also some lounge chairs and outdoor seating. The snack sized acai bowl is huge, what most other places would call medium, so while pricey, they do pack a ton in there.

Description: A Black woman-owned franchise location of a bright açaí bowl and smoothie shop serving customizable fruit bowls, pitaya bowls, smoothie blends, fresh fruit, granola, nuts, seeds, coconut, nut butters, honey, Nutella, and other toppings in a tropical-themed counter-service setting. The interior uses colorful branding, beach-inspired décor, casual dine-in seating, and a clean fast-casual layout built around the ordering counter, prep area, and fruit-forward display of bowls and smoothies.
Comments:
Park in the garage that's shared by all businesses in this area, its free. Add granola to your fruit bowl for a good crunch and taste that's kind of like cereal. The bowl sizes are very generous. The smallest size, snack, is to me what most places would consider medium. They pack a ton in there.

I like this location, which is but one of many in the area, specifically because it's black owned and I'm black. I'm unapologetically biased. There's not a ton of seating, but what they do have has personality. Tropical vibes all around. And there's some outdoor seating as well.

They have free Wi-Fi so you can hangout and work/study. Access to outlets is good. And if you need coffee or more space to camp out, there's a Waterbean next door. Also there's a Trader Joe's in this complex. Do with that what you will.

Comments:
Very small. There are 2 tables with chairs, a small bar-style seating area, a coffee table and armchairs in front of a faux fireplace, and then 2 more armchairs and a couch.

Small and compact, so this isn’t the place to come if you’re looking to have a super secret conversation. But the staff is nice, and in the same complex is 220C Bahn Mi & Coffee, which you can expand to if you find yourself without a seat here.

Comments:
Small space with some very comfortable armchairs and a nice tropical aesthetic. The bowls are huge. Even the smallest “snack” size is what many would consider at least a medium. There’s a fake fireplace which is a nice touch aesthetically. 

If you’re coming to hangout on your laptop, bring noise cancelling headphones. The music is kind of loud since it’s a small space. There’s a few outlets if you sit at the end of the booth style seating so you can stay charged up.

Address: 12820 S Tryon St Suite 100, Charlotte, NC 28273
Hours: ["Monday: 9 AM - 8 PM", "Tuesday: 9 AM - 8 PM", "Wednesday: 9 AM - 8 PM", "Thursday: 9 AM - 8 PM", "Friday: 9 AM - 8 PM", "Saturday: 9 AM - 8 PM", "Sunday: 9 AM - 8 PM"]
Description: A bright açaí bowl and smoothie shop serving customizable fruit bowls, pitaya bowls, smoothie blends, fresh fruit, granola, nuts, seeds, coconut, nut butters, honey, Nutella, and other toppings in a tropical-themed counter-service setting. The menu centers on organic açaí bases, dragon fruit blends, tropical fruit blends, protein blends, coconut bases, specialty bowls, build-your-own bowls, and blended drinks. The interior uses colorful branding, beach-inspired décor, casual dine-in seating, and a clean fast-casual layout built around the ordering counter, prep area, and fruit-forward display of bowls and smoothies.
Comments:
There’s a fireplace that I think is fake but I didn’t look close enough to be able to really tell. It’s a nice touch. The space has three four-person tables, a lounge area with a couch and two armchairs, and a tall bar style seating area with four chairs. Oh and some outdoor seating. So it’s small but they do all they can to provide seating options.

The tropical aesthetic is unique and distinctive. The acai bowls are pricey but they truly pack stuff in. Even the smallest size called “snack” to me was a ton of stuff. There’s free Wi-Fi and pretty good access to outlets from the four-person tables, so one could study/work remotely here. It’s a nice little place.

Description: A bright açaí bowl and smoothie shop serving customizable fruit bowls, pitaya bowls, smoothie blends, fresh fruit, granola, nuts, seeds, coconut, nut butters, honey, Nutella, and other toppings in a tropical-themed counter-service setting. The menu centers on organic açaí bases, dragon fruit blends, tropical fruit blends, protein blends, coconut bases, specialty bowls, build-your-own bowls, and blended drinks. The interior uses colorful branding, beach-inspired décor, casual dine-in seating, and a clean fast-casual layout built around the ordering counter, prep area, and fruit-forward display of bowls and smoothies.
Comments:
This location used to be a Waterbean, and if you ever visited it, just know that Palm Berries really changed things up with their re-design, and for the better! It's way less claustrophobic feeling, it's small but doesn't feel like you're on top of someone else sitting down. The menu is a lot of really healthy fruit stuff. Açaí was new to me when I first visited, I now know it's very great! Every kind of fruit and granola you can think of packed into a bowl. The Sunset Bowl is my favorite.

The space itself has a couch and two lounge chairs, then a long wall of booth style seating with chairs across from them. Then a few outdoor tables as well, so not a ton of space, but enough to feel reasonably confident you'll always have a place to plop down, especially on a warm day.

The owners of this franchise location gave me a speech about how their açaí comes straight from Brazil and they don't cut or water it down or do anything to it, and all the fruit is cut fresh daily. All I know is what I ate tasted incredible, so I believe them. You can come here to work remote or study, just bring noise cancelling headphones to lock in. It's small so you can hear everything going on inside. Also, the staff is really nice.

Hours: ["Monday: 6 AM - 8 PM", "Tuesday: 6 AM - 8 PM", "Wednesday: 6 AM - 8 PM", "Thursday: 6 AM - 8 PM", "Friday: 6 AM - 8 PM", "Saturday: 6 AM - 8 PM", "Sunday: 7:30 AM - 7 PM"]
Description:
A Brazilian café and snack bar in Charlotte serving coffee, Brazilian pastries, breakfast breads, savory snacks, sweets, smoothies, juices, tapioca wraps, sandwiches, burgers, and casual café meals. The menu includes Brazilian favorites such as pão de queijo, coxinha, pastel, risoles, ham and cheese melts, cakes, brigadeiro-style sweets, espresso drinks, and fresh drinks.

The space has a casual, colorful café feel with counter service, family-recipe cooking, Brazilian cultural details, Wi-Fi, and a menu that works for breakfast, lunch, dessert, coffee, takeout, or a relaxed sit-down visit. Good for trying Brazilian comfort food, grabbing coffee and pastries, meeting friends, casual meals, family visits, and exploring a locally rooted café with a strong Brazilian identity.
Comments: The menu has so much stuff on it I've never heard of, I had to ask for more time to choose twice during my first visit, there's tons to explore! Bright and airy aesthetic, yellow metal chairs and patterned black marble tables, plus a few comfier lounge style chairs along a wall. I wouldn't say it's cozy but it's clean and well designed. There's a ton of food options, but also coffee, tea, and a lot of pastries. Everything's authentic, the staff, the TV's showing news or sports in Portuguese, even the menu. Which makes sense, since it's owned by a Brazilian mother-daughter team.

Hours: ["Monday: 7:30 AM - 3:30 PM", "Tuesday: 7:30 AM - 3 PM", "Wednesday: 7:30 AM - 3 PM", "Thursday: 7:30 AM - 3 PM", "Friday: 7:30 AM - 3 PM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 3 PM", "Sunday: 8 AM - 3 PM"]
Description:
A retro coffee shop and vinyl listening lounge near NoDa with a cozy, music-first feel and a slower pace than a typical grab-and-go cafe. The space is built for lingering, with records, turntables, lounge seating, and a warm listening-room atmosphere that makes it feel more like a friend’s well-curated living room than a standard coffee counter.

During the day, it works as a relaxed spot for coffee, tea, baked goods, light bites, reading, conversation, or easing into a work session with music in the background. As the concept grows into evening service, the vibe shifts toward a low-key cocktail lounge where guests can settle in, browse records, listen intentionally, and hang out before or after visiting other spots at The Pass.
Comments:
If you’ve grown tired of cookie-cutter, industrial-chic coffee shops, with their unfinished floors, hard metal chairs, exposed brick and distressed-wood counters, this is the place for you. It is, in almost every way, the opposite of that.  

The room has personality, with a bright, retro 1970s palette, plenty of natural light, bar-style seating, two four-person tables, three two-person tables and an Eames Lounge Chair and Ottoman. Plus, a sitting area in the back has a couch and several chairs, which is a great spot to plop down with a group of friends, or read a book.

I don't drink coffee so I can't tell you if it's good or not, but they have all the things. The many kinds of coffee, hot or cold, matcha lattes, herbal teas, and pastries. The real draw here is the music though. They have curated vinyl displays, listening nights, record swaps, and portable Sound Burger record players.  

Parking is free, which unfortunately is kind of rare in NoDa. For remote work, it's a good option. Access to outlets is good at most seating spots, and the natural light makes it an energetic space to hangout in. I like this place!

Hours: ["Monday: Closed", "Tuesday: Closed", "Wednesday: 7 AM - 11 AM", "Thursday: 7 AM - 11 AM", "Friday: 7 AM - 11 AM", "Saturday: 7 AM - 11 AM", "Sunday: Closed"]
Description: A Ukrainian-owned espresso bar and coffee pop-up inside a dine-in Plaza Midwood restaurant space, serving espresso drinks, chai, matcha, lemonade, teas, and seasonal specialty lattes. The setup combines a mobile coffee cart background with a fixed morning coffee service, giving visitors a place to grab coffee and sit within the host restaurant’s dining environment. Good for morning coffee, casual conversation, specialty drinks, quick meetups, and a short relaxed stop rather than an all-day café or dedicated remote-work space.
Comments: This is inside Pure Pizza. It’s super small, but they pack seating into the building pretty well for the size. Around 3 2-person tables then 10 or so bar style seats at counters, and an outdoor bench. I don’t drink coffee so I don’t know what to tell you about the taste. It looks good at least. This certainly isn’t a place to camp out on a laptop and study or work. More so a morning hangout spot for a quick meeting, or solo date with a book.

Address: 222 West Blvd Ste S112, Charlotte, NC 28203
Hours: ["Monday: Closed", "Tuesday: Closed", "Wednesday: Closed", "Thursday: 11 AM - 7 PM", "Friday: 11 AM - 7 PM", "Saturday: 11 AM - 7 PM", "Sunday: 11 AM - 7 PM"]
Description: A small, pink, photo-friendly, Black-woman owned coffee shop inside an engraved and permanent jewelry studio in South End. The menu centers on coffee and matcha drinks, with a boutique-like setting that works well for friend dates, quick daytime visits, content stops, and jewelry appointments. Visitors can walk in during café hours or book appointments for welded or clasped jewelry, charms, chains, and same-day engraving. Expect a cute, upbeat, highly styled, unapologetically feminine space.
Comments:
Check out this excellent YouTube walkthrough of the place to get the full rundown. It really does look like it could’ve been a set for the Barbie movie. They proudly market themselves as the “Girliest Coffee in Charlotte,” and I’d say that’s accurate. No doubt about it. Glowgirl is 2 things in 1 space. Glowgirl is the jewelry store, and Glowgirl Cafe is the coffee shop. The whole place is pink, playful, feminine, and very much committed to the theme.

The owner, Jorgie Cooper, is a St. Louis native who moved to Charlotte in 2018 to work at Wells Fargo. She worked there for 6 years before eventually creating the jewelry company and then the cafe. She loves pink and feels it’s the best of all colors, which makes complete sense once you see the space.

I also love the St. Louis connection because I spent 6 years in St. Louis for undergrad and my master’s. I loved my time there so much and was so embraced by the community that I consider it home more than anywhere else on earth. Glowgirl is of course open to everyone, but Jorgie isn’t shy about it being curated for ladies to feel welcome, seen, and loved!

Address: 2410 Herrons Nest Pl Nw #103, Concord, NC 28027
Hours: ["Monday: 6 AM - 11 PM", "Tuesday: 6 AM - 11 PM", "Wednesday: 6 AM - 11 PM", "Thursday: 6 AM - 11 PM", "Friday: 6 AM - 12 AM", "Saturday: 7 AM - 12 AM", "Sunday: 7 AM - 11 PM"]
Description: A Yemeni-style coffee house in Concord serving traditional spiced coffee, espresso drinks, teas, refreshers, pastries, cakes, and specialty lattes in a warm modern café setting. The menu features drinks such as Harazi coffee, Sana’ani coffee, Turkish coffee, Adeni/Karak chai, qishr, pistachio lattes, cardamom-forward coffee drinks, and sweet café desserts. The atmosphere blends Middle Eastern coffee culture with a contemporary coffee shop feel, making it good for coffee dates, casual meetups, dessert, conversation, evening hangs, and trying Yemeni coffee traditions in the Concord area.
Comments:
If you've been to one Haraz location, you've just about been to them all in terms of aesthetic. Yet, this place is just a little bit less polished and fancy looking than their other locations. Not by much, just slightly, but as someone that's been to all Haraz locations in the greater Charlotte area, it's noticeable to me. Aside from that, it's all the same menu items, and it's a great place to plop down with a laptop and work remotely, or study, or just hangout.

Acoustically it can get pretty loud when it's filled as the space itself isn't too spread out, so bring noise cancelling headphones. The chairs here are different than other Haraz locations. They have a curve to the bottom part, like it's acquiescing to the shape of your bum. I don't know if I like that. Access to outlets is decent, not great, not terrible.

Tags: Charlotte Local,Black Owned,Woman Owned,Loose Leaf Tea
Hours: ["Monday: 7 AM - 6 PM", "Tuesday: 7 AM - 6 PM", "Wednesday: 7 AM - 6 PM", "Thursday: 7 AM - 6 PM", "Friday: 7 AM - 6 PM", "Saturday: 9 AM - 2 PM", "Sunday: Closed"]
Description: A Black woman-owned modern coffee shop in Belmont serving coffee, tea, fresh juices, matcha, smoothies, and pastries. The storefront is designed as a neighborhood cafe where guests can grab something to go, meet friends, or settle in with a laptop. Expect a warm, polished space with a connection-focused vibe and a straightforward menu centered on drinks and baked goods.
Comments:
It smells good here, and it's very clean. The aesthetic is calming, like a warm hug, or 2000s Neo-Soul music (we're talking India Arie, Musiq Soulchild, D'Angelo). There are streaks of green turf in the floor that helps break up the tiles, which I think is a nice touch. The lighting is soft. If you've ever felt like most coffee shops these days have harsh overhead lighting, this is the opposite. The seats are plush and comfy, not hard metal or wood, but something you could sit in for a while without your back hurting. Even the tables are sturdy and clearly not cheap. Everything here seems intentional and done with care. It's far from cookie cutter aesthetically.

I don't drink coffee, so I can't tell you how it tastes, but they have a marvelous selection of herbal teas that I enjoy, as well as pastries, including a red velvet cinnamon roll that appears from time to time. It's owned by a black woman, which being black myself, brings me joy. She intentionally wants the space to be inviting for remote workers, which as a remote worker myself, brings me even more joy.

Description:
An artisan French bakery and café in downtown Huntersville serving handcrafted pastries, breads, croissants, cakes, coffee drinks, teas, breakfast items, sandwiches, quiche, and light lunch options. The bakery focuses on traditional French techniques, imported French flour, European ingredients, seasonal ingredients, and small-batch baked goods.

The menu includes croissants, pain au chocolat, baguettes, brioche, croque monsieur, French baguette sandwiches, quiche, espresso drinks, signature lattes, chai, and teas. Good for morning coffee, pastry runs, breakfast, lunch, relaxed conversation, quick pickup, dessert, and a European-style bakery stop in the Lake Norman area.
Comments: This Instagram post has an announcement of what will be their second location. More details here as well.

Address: 378 Technology Center Way #1120, Rock Hill, SC 29730
Hours: ["Monday: Closed", "Tuesday: 11 AM - 10 PM", "Wednesday: 11 AM - 10 PM", "Thursday: 11 AM - 10 PM", "Friday: 11 AM - 11 PM", "Saturday: 11 AM - 11 PM", "Sunday: 11 AM - 6 PM"]
Description: A neighborhood taproom, specialty market, and casual gathering space inside Rock Hill’s Power House development, offering craft beer, curated wines, gastropub fare, gourmet Italian sandwiches, artisan brats, charcuterie boards, shareable appetizers, grab-and-go meals, local goods, and market-style food and drink items. The space combines indoor seating, patio seating, a beer garden feel, and a community-focused event calendar with live music, wine tastings, beer festivals, vendor pop-ups, private gatherings, and social events. Good for after-work drinks, casual meals, meeting friends, browsing local goods, trying wine or beer, attending community events, and spending time in one of Rock Hill’s mixed-use dining and entertainment districts.
Comments: Lots of seating, indoor and outdoors. This is a part of a larger venue called Power House which has other businesses, including coffee shops and restaurants, so there’s plenty to explore here. You will always find a place to sit.

Address: 378 Technology Center Way Center, Rock Hill, SC 29730
Hours: ["Monday: 9 AM - 6 PM", "Tuesday: 9 AM - 6 PM", "Wednesday: 9 AM - 6 PM", "Thursday: 9 AM - 6 PM", "Friday: 9 AM - 6 PM", "Saturday: 9 AM - 2 PM", "Sunday: 9 AM - 2 PM"]
Description: A Filipino-inspired coffee shop inside Rock Hill’s Power House development serving classic espresso drinks, coffee sourced from the Philippines, ube beverages, calamansi-based Filipino sweet tea, and Filipino pastries in a creative, culture-forward setting. The space blends a coffee bar with an artful, hip-hop-influenced atmosphere, making it useful for coffee runs, casual meetups, specialty drinks, and spending time around the larger Power House dining, retail, and event district. Good for trying Filipino-inspired drinks, grabbing espresso, meeting friends, and supporting a locally rooted coffee concept with a strong cultural identity.
Comments:
I don't know of any other place in the greater Charlotte area specifically focused on serving Filipino style coffee and cinnamon rolls ❤️. I am so happy about both of those things. I grew up with a Filipino friend whose mom would make us lumpia (like an egg roll but Filipino style) whenever we'd hangout, and it was marvelous. I miss those times. Or maybe it's the food I miss, I don't know. But what I do know is this place is powered by Filipino coffee and pastries, so if you've ever wanted to try either, this is the place to visit.

The coffee shop itself is just a small window, but it's inside a larger development called Power House, which has apartments, restaurants, plenty of indoor and outdoor seating, and another coffee shop called Mug and Maple. I think it's a bit funny that the same floor in the building has two coffee shops, seems a strange decision from management to put them both so close, but it is what it is.

There's plenty of space to hangout and work on your laptop here, and an area with a couch if you want to settle in and read a book. There's also outdoor seating and a restaurant in the building if you get hungry. Parking is free, there's a garage just for visitors.

Address: 378 Technology Center Way, Rock Hill, SC 29730
Hours: ["Monday: 7 AM - 4 PM", "Tuesday: 7 AM - 4 PM", "Wednesday: 7 AM - 4 PM", "Thursday: 7 AM - 4 PM", "Friday: 7 AM - 4 PM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 4 PM", "Sunday: 8 AM - 3 PM"]
Description:
A health-conscious coffee shop and café in Rock Hill’s Power House development serving specialty coffee, espresso drinks, lattes, breakfast items, lunch sandwiches, bowls, pastries, and real café food made without seed oils, bleached grains, or artificial ingredients. The coffee program emphasizes carefully sourced beans, locally roasted coffee, organic flavorings, local honey, New England maple syrup, and signature drinks like maple, mocha, honey, and brown butter lattes.

The interior is designed with an upscale ski-lodge feel, featuring a long granite bar, cement floors, couches, chairs, cozy seating areas, and workspace-friendly spots for lingering. Good for coffee dates, laptop work, breakfast, lunch, casual meetings, weekend visits, and anyone looking for a polished café with both strong coffee and a more ingredient-conscious food menu. Outdoor patio seating is also publicly listed as dog-friendly.
Comments:
On Tuesdays and Thursdays, they "roll the dice", literally a gigantic pair of dice, and if it lands on a certain number chosen by the barista then you get your drink for free. I haven't yet gotten a free drink, but with the way gas prices are these days, I appreciate the gesture. Aesthetically the space is industrial chic, but unlike most places using that style, they have some comfy couches for reading, feeling cozy, and staying awhile.

Coffee, tea, pastries, food, and plenty of space. Both indoor and outdoor there's a lot of seating, partially because this coffee shop is inside a larger development called Power House, which has apartments, a food hall and all sorts of other stuff inside the building.

There's a general seating area outside the coffee shop for all guests, but the shop itself has plenty of seating inside its own space. The staff is very friendly, if you want to just yap at the coffee bar, they're down for it. Parking is free, there's a garage in the complex, you might have to drive around to find it. One garage is for residents only while the other garage is for visitors.

Address: 101 W Morehead St Ste A, Charlotte, NC 28202
Hours: ["Monday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Tuesday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Wednesday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Thursday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Friday: 8 AM - 6 PM", "Saturday: 10 AM - 6 PM", "Sunday: 10 AM - 2 PM"]
Description:
A locally owned Charlotte café focused on certified organic coffee, fair-trade sourcing, fresh local produce, and environmentally conscious operations. The menu includes hot and iced espresso drinks, lattes, matcha, chai, tea, hot chocolate, breakfast sandwiches, avocado toast, grilled cheese, BLT, caprese, chicken pesto sandwiches, turkey and Swiss sandwiches, wraps, burrito bowls, Caesar salads, smoothies, pastries, and seasonal treats.

The café positions itself around sustainability, zero-waste practices, recyclable packaging, composting, and community-focused service. Good for coffee, breakfast, lunch, smoothies, casual meetups, light meals, and a healthier café stop with vegetarian-friendly options and made-to-order food.
Comments:
Great coffee, great pastries, great smoothies, but the highlight for me is the food. The chicken pesto sandwich in particular is marvelous. And the breakfast sandwiches are great as well. You can add a hash brown to each for extra crunch. The atmosphere is clean, polished, professional, and bright. Lots of windows, so plenty of natural light.

It's small, no more than 4–5 tables, but it seems to get most of its business from the adjacent office building, where people pop in and pop out, so if you're looking to camp out on your laptop, you should be able to find a corner provided you come before or after the lunch rush. The staff is very kind. It's owned by an Ethiopian family, hence the Black-owned and Habesha tags on this place, and friends and family all help run the place.

Outlet access is good from tables in the corners. I've worked remote here for a few hours and enjoyed it. The only downside is parking, which is in an attached garage. You'll have to pay, so make the most of your time and walk around the neighborhood. It's a walkable neighborhood with a few other businesses nearby to explore.

Hours: ["Monday: 7 AM - 3 PM", "Tuesday: 7 AM - 3 PM", "Wednesday: 7 AM - 3 PM", "Thursday: 7 AM - 3 PM", "Friday: 7 AM - 3 PM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 4 PM", "Sunday: 8 AM - 4 PM"]
Description: A specialty coffee shop inside the Queensbridge Collective development, serving espresso drinks, drip coffee, seasonal lattes, teas, pastries, breads, loaves, and café food from a Charlotte coffee group known for thoughtful sourcing and made-from-scratch ingredients. The space is designed as a polished South End gathering spot with a modern café feel, built for coffee runs, casual meetings, remote work, relaxed conversation, and visits before or after time around nearby apartments, offices, restaurants, and entertainment.
Comments:
Very small. The coffee shop has a corner with some inside seating consisting of hard wooden chairs, and then some outdoor seating, but it's not a ton. It's very bright though. The corner it's in has very tall windows, so certainly a good place if you're trying to stay awake and energetic. Access to outlets is challenging. Wi-Fi is free and fast though. Parking is in a paid garage, but they'll validate it up to a certain time limit.

And just as a matter of educating the Charlotte community, Night Swim as a brand exists as a collaboration between Not Just Coffee and Undercurrent Coffee. So, it's like both of the teams from those coffee shops put together. I've found Night Swim tends to have better bagels with more generous cream cheese servings than either coffee shop brand on its own. I don't know if that matters to you, but now you know.

Address: 4808 Central Ave Suit H G, Charlotte, NC 28205
Hours: ["Monday: 8 AM - 8 PM", "Tuesday: 8 AM - 8 PM", "Wednesday: 8 AM - 8 PM", "Thursday: 8 AM - 8 PM", "Friday: 8 AM - 8 PM", "Saturday: 8 AM - 8 PM", "Sunday: 8 AM - 8 PM"]
Description:
A casual Charlotte restaurant and café serving breakfast sandwiches, breakfast platters, smashburgers, Yemeni coffee drinks, espresso beverages, spiced teas, and lunch and dinner plates in a bright counter-service setting. The menu includes egg and cheese sandwiches on rolls, bagels, or hoagie bread, home fries, single, double, and triple smashburgers, a patty-melt-style butter burger, fries, chicken over rice, fried fish with rice and vegetables, chicken shawarma wraps, chopped cheese, Philly cheesesteak, and chicken Parmesan sandwiches.

The coffee side offers Arabic coffee, Yemeni coffee with cardamom and cream, coffee husk drinks brewed with ginger, cinnamon, and cream, cappuccino, specialty lattes, Yemeni red tea with cardamom, Moroccan mint tea, and creamy Adeni chai. Inside, the dining room has booth seating, dark tables, warm wood-look floors, digital menu boards, and decorative accent walls with black, gold, green, and wood-slat details. Good for breakfast, burgers, coffee, casual meals, trying Yemeni drinks, takeout, delivery, and relaxed sit-down dining and vibes.
Comments: Read all about this place here. It’s very well designed, one side is the restaurant seating and the other is a large room set up coffee shop style with plenty of spots to plop down with a laptop and camp out.

Description: A cafe-banking hybrid in South End with coffee, casual workspace seating, banking support, and a low-pressure place to study, meet, or work remotely. The large standalone brick space sits near the Bland Street light rail station and works well for laptop use with free Wi-Fi, comfortable seating, and semi-private spots for focused conversations. Visitors can grab handcrafted coffee or tea, use the walk-up ATM, talk with cafe ambassadors about banking or financial questions, or settle in for a break without needing to be a Capital One customer.
Comments: I've been to one of these in New York. They're actually pretty nice, it's a coffee shop but if you're a Capital One customer you get some discounts, and usually they have little one person booths you can use as a Capital One customer for remote work. More details here. Looking forward to this one opening in Charlotte.
